title: How much to trust an AI is a guess you can get better at, not just a feeling
Trusting an AI is a careful guess, not a feeling
Trust is a calibrated forecast, not a feeling
Trust is a calibrated forecast, not a vibe
Trust: a calibrated forecast, not a feeling
lede: Sometimes an AI gives you an answer that sounds sure of itself and reads really well, so part of you just wants to believe it. Stop for a second and ask where that nice feeling is coming from — the smooth words, or any real proof? Writing well is just a style. A computer can write something that sounds lovely and is still wrong, all in the same sentence. So how smooth it sounds does not tell you if it is true.

An AI gives you a confident, well-written answer and you feel like trusting it. Pause and notice where that feeling is coming from — how it was written, or the facts? Fluency is only a writing style. A model can sound smooth and still be wrong in the same sentence, so the smoothness tells you little about whether the claim is true.

An AI gives you a confident, well-written answer and you feel like trusting it. Stop and notice where that feeling comes from — the prose, or the facts? Fluency is a writing style. A model can be smooth and wrong in the same sentence, so the smoothness tells you little about whether the claim holds.

An AI gives a confident, polished answer and you feel like trusting it. Notice the source — prose, or facts? Fluency is a style. A model can be smooth and wrong in one sentence; smoothness says little about whether the claim holds.
Confidence and polish trigger trust. But the feeling tracks prose, not facts. Fluency is style. Smooth and wrong coexist; smoothness is no signal of correctness.
forecast: Here is one way you could try instead. Rather than asking "do I trust this?", treat trust like a weather forecast that you keep adjusting. Pick just one thing in the answer you can actually check — a number, a name, a date, or a step that either works or it doesn't. Test only that one thing. If it turns out right, trust the answer a little more. If it turns out wrong, trust it a lot less. You are not trying to be totally sure. You are just keeping a rough sense of how likely it is to be right, and changing that sense as you learn more. The real trick is to make each check quick and easy, so you keep doing it — instead of checking once and then never bothering again. Not everything in an answer is checkable, of course, so see whether this rough sense ends up serving you better than a gut feeling you never question.

Try treating trust as a forecast instead. Pick one thing in the answer you can check — a number, a name, a date, or a step that either works or doesn't. Test just that one. If it holds, raise your estimate a little; if it cracks, drop it sharply. You are not after certainty, just a likelihood you keep updating as evidence arrives. The trick is keeping each check cheap enough that you actually keep doing it, rather than checking once and then coasting. Not every claim is checkable, so see whether this beats trusting your gut.

So try treating trust as a forecast. Pick one checkable claim in the output — a number, a name, a date, a step that either works or doesn't. Probe just that one. If it holds, nudge your estimate up a notch; if it cracks, drop it hard. The goal isn't certainty, it's a probability you keep updating as evidence comes in. The trick is keeping each check cheap enough that you actually keep doing it, instead of verifying once and then coasting. Not every claim is checkable, so see whether this serves you better than a gut read.

Try trust as a forecast. Probe one checkable claim — number, name, date, or pass/fail step. Holds: nudge up. Cracks: drop hard. Aim for an updating probability, not certainty. Keep checks cheap so they recur. Test whether it beats a gut read.
habits: This works together with two other ideas. First, it is how you actually do Delegate the task, keep the judgment — you look at the answer and weigh it up yourself, instead of just going along with it. Second, the claims that are easiest to check are the ones that say something clear and specific. That is because A good explanation is hard to vary, so it is easy to spot where it would fall apart.

This fits with two habits. It is how you actually do Delegate the task, keep the judgment — you weigh the answer rather than just defer to it. And the easiest claims to check are the ones that commit to something specific, because A good explanation is hard to vary, so it is easy to test where it would fail.

This pairs with two habits. It's how Delegate the task, keep the judgment in practice — you weigh the output rather than defer to it. And the claims easiest to probe are the ones that commit to something, because A good explanation is hard to vary and so it's easy to test where it would fail.

This pairs with two habits. It's Delegate the task, keep the judgment in practice — you weigh the output, not defer to it. And the most probeable claims commit to something, because A good explanation is hard to vary, so its failure points are easy to test.
Pairs with two habits: Delegate the task, keep the judgment (weigh, don't defer), and probe committed claims, since A good explanation is hard to vary and its failure points are testable.
next: Here is one small thing to try. The next time an AI gives you an answer, pick just one thing in it and go check whether that one thing is really true.
